title: Designing for Local vs. Global Context
concept: Good CNN design usually widens context gradually — early layers focus on local details, while later layers and operations like pooling combine those details into a broader view of the scene.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why isn't the biggest possible receptive field always the best choice?

options.text: Because some tasks still need sharp local detail, not just broad context
Because large receptive fields are illegal in CNNs
Because receptive fields only matter in language models

exercises.explain: Exactly — strong vision systems balance local detail with wider context.

cards.title: Start close, zoom out later
cards.body: Good CNNs usually do not try to understand the whole scene in the very first layer. They begin with small local clues, then widen the view over time so later layers can combine those clues into bigger ideas.

cards.title: Designing the growth
cards.body: Architecture design is partly about deciding how quickly receptive fields should grow. Stacked convolutions, and sometimes pooling or stride, gradually widen what later activations can see.

cards.title: Puzzle pieces before the full picture
cards.body: When you solve a puzzle, you first notice individual pieces, colors, and edges. Only later do those pieces connect into a castle, a dragon, or a sunset scene. CNNs often grow context the same way.

cards.title: Edges, then eyes, then faces
cards.body: Early layers may detect tiny edges or textures. After more layers — and sometimes pooling to summarize nearby regions — later layers can use wider receptive fields to recognize an eye, then a face, then a whole person in the scene.

cards.title: It's not local or global
cards.body: A strong architecture does not pick one scale and stick with it forever. It usually mixes both: local detail early, broader context later, with receptive fields widening across depth.

cards.title: Early Layers vs. Later Layers
cards.body: Many CNNs widen context gradually instead of all at once:

left.text:
Small local patches
edges, corners, textures
fine detail matters

right.text:
Wider receptive fields
parts combined into larger
scene-level understanding

exercises.prompt: Which architecture plan best balances local and global context?

options.text: Start with local layers, then gradually widen context with more layers and sometimes pooling or stride
Force the first layer to see the whole image and never change scale again
Remove all later layers so context never grows
Choose filter sizes randomly and hope for the best

exercises.explain: Exactly — many CNNs build from local clues toward broader understanding.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Good CNNs usually make every layer look at the whole image from the very beginning.
exercises.explain: False — early layers usually focus on local detail, and wider context appears later.

exercises.sentence: Many CNNs widen context ___, not all at once.
exercises.explain: Gradually — that lets the network keep local detail while building toward larger patterns.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures good receptive-field design?

options.text: Early layers often focus on local details, while later layers combine them using wider context
Later layers should know less than earlier layers about the scene
Pooling exists only to make lesson files shorter

exercises.explain: Right — architecture design is about controlling how local clues turn into bigger-picture understanding.

exercises.prompt: Why do CNN architects often let receptive fields widen gradually across layers instead of making every neuron see the whole image immediately?

options.text: Because early layers can lock onto simple local parts first, and later layers can use wider context to combine those parts into larger meaningful patterns
Because later layers should ignore earlier details completely
Because receptive fields have nothing to do with architecture design
Because only the last layer is allowed to look at pixels

exercises.explain: Exactly. Good CNNs often widen context step by step so simple local clues can grow into larger patterns and objects. Next up: feature hierarchies — how those local edges and parts stack into full visual concepts.
